perturbation.original_text: 7. Miscellaneous. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Rights. No rights or licenses to trademarks, inventions, copyrights, patents or any other intellectual property rights are implied or granted under this Agreement. Subject to the obligations of this Agreement, neither party will be precluded from independently developing technology or pursuing business opportunities similar to those covered by the Confidential Information disclosed hereunder. Each party retains sole discretion to assign or reassign the job responsibilities of its employees. Each party acknowledges that the other party may currently or in the future be developing information internally, or receiving information from third parties, that is similar to the Confidential Information. Nothing in this Agreement will prohibit Recipient from developing or having developed for it products, concepts, systems or techniques that are similar to those contemplated by or embodied in the Confidential Information, provided the Recipient does not violate its obligations under this Agreement. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law clause is moved from the 'Miscellaneous' section to the 'Rights' section, where it is less likely to be noticed. This creates ambiguity about which jurisdiction's laws govern the contract, potentially hindering its enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1646 states that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed, or, if it indicates a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. Moving the choice of law clause to an unrelated section obscures its importance and may lead to disputes regarding the applicable law, contradicting the principle of clear contractual interpretation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text moves the governing law clause, a critical component of the contract, from a standard 'Miscellaneous' section to the 'Rights' section. This relocation obscures the clause, potentially leading to disputes about which jurisdiction's laws apply, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation states that this obscures the clause's importance. California Civil Code § 1646, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifies that contracts should be interpreted according to the law of the place of performance or creation if the place of performance is not indicated. By obscuring the governing law clause, the modified text introduces ambiguity about the applicable law, making the contract's interpretation and enforcement less clear and thus creating a contradiction under Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient hereby acknowledges and agrees that no remedy at law will afford Discloser adequate protection against, or appropriate compensation for, breach of Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement. Accordingly, Recipient agrees that Discloser shall be entitled to specific performance of Recipient’s obligations, as well as such further equitable relief as may be granted by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Exclusions. “Confidential Information” excludes information which: (a) is now or hereafter becomes generally known or available, through no act or failure to act on Recipient’s part; (b) Recipient independently knows at the time of receiving such information; (c) a third party hereafter furnishes to Recipient without restriction on disclosure and without breach of any confidentiality obligations; (d) Recipient has independently developed without using any Confidential Information or breaching this Agreement; or (e) Discloser gives written permission to Recipient to disclose. Recipient hereby acknowledges and agrees that no remedy at law will afford Discloser adequate protection against, or appropriate compensation for, breach of Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement. Accordingly, Recipient agrees that Discloser shall be entitled to specific performance of Recipient’s obligations, as well as such further equitable relief as may be granted by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: The clause about specific performance and equitable relief has been moved from the 'Miscellaneous' section to the 'Exclusions' section. Specific performance is a remedy, and it's placement within the definition of what is excluded as confidential information is misplaced. This obscures its purpose and may make it difficult for the Discloser to enforce this right.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3366 states that specific performance may be compelled. By placing the clause within the 'Exclusions' section, it creates confusion as to whether specific performance is indeed a valid remedy, potentially undermining its enforceability. Its new location implies it is an exclusion, when it is actually providing a remedy for Discloser.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3366 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. General Principles [3366 - 3369] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) 3366. Specific or preventive relief may be given as provided by the laws of this state. (Amended by Stats. 1905, Ch. 465.)
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly states that Discloser is entitled to specific performance, while moving this clause into the 'Exclusions' section creates ambiguity and undermines its enforceability. According to the law explanation, this placement implies it is an exclusion, when it actually provides a remedy for Discloser. Snippet 1 states "Specific or preventive relief may be given as provided by the laws of this state.", so it is clear that the original contract provided an avenue for this relief. Moving the clause harms the discloser, and misrepresents what specific performance actually means, creating a structural flaw.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Term and Termination. This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for a period of three (3) years unless terminated sooner as set forth below. This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon thirty (30) days written notice, provided however, that Paragraphs 3, 4, 5, and 7 shall survive any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Definition. Subject to the limitations set forth in Section 2, all information (including but not limited to ideas, techniques, works of authorship, models, inventions, know-how, processes, equipment, algorithms, software source documents and formulae related to the current, future, and proposed products and services of each of the parties and information concerning research, development, financial information, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, customer lists, business forecasts, sales and merchandising, and marketing plans), disclosed by one party (the “Discloser”) to the other party (the “Recipient”) which is labeled or marked “Confidential” or with some other similar proprietary legend, or which, in the context of the disclosure, the Recipient has reason to believe is confidential, shall be deemed to be “Confidential Information.” If Confidential Information is disclosed in oral form, the Discloser shall identify it as confidential at the time of disclosure and thereafter summarize it in writing and transmit such summary to the Recipient within thirty (30) days of the oral disclosure. This Agreement shall remain in full force and effect for a period of three (3) years unless terminated sooner as set forth below. This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon thirty (30) days written notice, provided however, that Paragraphs 3, 4, 5, and 7 shall survive any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Term and Termination' clause to the 'Definition' section obscures the duration of the agreement. A reasonable party would expect to find the term and termination information in a section that explains it, not in a section dedicated to defining the subject matter of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1654 states that in cases of uncertainty not removed by the preceding rules, the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist. Placing the term and termination details in an unusual location creates ambiguity about the agreement's duration, which is typically resolved against the drafter. By reducing the visibility of the term, the ambiguity is heightened.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1654. In cases of uncertainty not removed by the preceding rules, the language of a contract should be interpreted most strongly against the party who caused the uncertainty to exist. (Amended by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a structural flaw by placing the 'Term and Termination' clause within the 'Definition' section, obscuring the agreement's duration. This contradicts California Civil Code § 1654, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that uncertainties in a contract should be interpreted against the party causing the uncertainty. By placing the term and termination details in an unusual location, the changed text creates ambiguity, thus undermining the clarity and enforceability of a key contractual element and resulting in a structural flaw-legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Restrictions/Obligations. Recipient shall: (a) only disclose the Confidential Information to those employees and contractors with a need to know; provided that Recipient binds those employees and contractors to terms at least as restrictive as those stated in this Agreement (or, with respect to professional advisors, advises them of its obligations with respect to the Confidential Information); (b) not disclose any Confidential Information to any third party, without Discloser’s prior written consent; (c) use such Confidential Information only to the extent required to accomplish the Purpose; (d) not reproduce Confidential Information in any form except as required to accomplish the Purpose; (e) not reverse engineer, decompile, or disassemble any software disclosed by the Discloser; (f) not directly or indirectly export or transmit any Confidential Information to any country to which such export or transmission is restricted by regulation or statute; and (g) promptly provide the Discloser with notice of any actual or threatened breach of the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Ownership and Return of Confidential Information. All Confidential Information (including all copies thereof) shall remain the property of Discloser. All copies shall bear all proprietary markings contained on or in the originals. Upon the request of Discloser, Recipient shall, at Recipient’s option, either (a) return such materials to Discloser, or (b) certify the destruction thereof. Recipient shall: (a) only disclose the Confidential Information to those employees and contractors with a need to know; provided that Recipient binds those employees and contractors to terms at least as restrictive as those stated in this Agreement (or, with respect to professional advisors, advises them of its obligations with respect to the Confidential Information); (b) not disclose any Confidential Information to any third party, without Discloser’s prior written consent; (c) use such Confidential Information only to the extent required to accomplish the Purpose; (d) not reproduce Confidential Information in any form except as required to accomplish the Purpose; (e) not reverse engineer, decompile, or disassemble any software disclosed by the Discloser; (f) not directly or indirectly export or transmit any Confidential Information to any country to which such export or transmission is restricted by regulation or statute; and (g) promptly provide the Discloser with notice of any actual or threatened breach of the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the obligations/restrictions placed on the Recipient within the section about 'Ownership and Return of Confidential Information' creates a structural flaw that lessens the visibility of the duties of the Recipient. It would be harder to determine the standard of care required by Recipient.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While confidentiality agreements are not generally considered restraints of trade, the specific restrictions within must be reasonable and narrowly tailored. Hiding such terms within a seemingly unrelated section like 'Ownership and Return' makes it harder to assess if the restrictions are reasonable, potentially challenging its enforceability under § 16600.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that moving the obligations/restrictions to a section about ownership creates a structural flaw, making it harder to determine the standard of care required. The law explanation states that California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business, and the specific restrictions must be reasonable and narrowly tailored. Hiding such terms within a seemingly unrelated section makes it harder to assess if the restrictions are reasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a) states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." By obscuring the obligations within an unrelated section, it weakens the enforceability, and undermines the spirit of BPC 16600, creating a contradiction.
